IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses the important problem of lexical entailment in context and proposes a practical method to derive contextualized word-in-context representations from type embeddings. It shows significant empirical gains over context-agnostic baselines on two novel datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB and CONTEXT-WN), succeeds in a cross-lingual setting, and establishes a new state-of-the-art on the Shwartz and Dagan (2015) dataset using only features induced from raw text. These results and the introduction of contextual similarity features are likely to influence follow-up work and motivate further research on contextualized approaches for lexical semantics.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experimental results on CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN and a cross-lingual dataset, and by the reported new state-of-the-art on the Shwartz and Dagan (2015) benchmark. The methodological choices (transforming type embeddings via context-derived masks, using similarity features, and comparing to context-agnostic and prior baselines including Context2Vec) are reasonable and experiments use standard metrics. However, the paper does not report statistical significance testing and notes minimal classifier tuning (only class weights in one experiment), which are minor weaknesses in the evaluation. Overall the technical evidence largely supports the claims, with only minor methodological gaps.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments are broad: evaluation on two novel datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN), a cross-lingual set, and an existing benchmark with reported state-of-the-art, and hyperparameters are reported. Related work is described as thorough and the paper explicitly discusses limitations and some negative results (e.g., Context2Vec performs poorly, lack of classifier tuning except class weights). However, the summary does not indicate systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses of the proposed feature components nor availability of code, leaving some reproducibility and component-level assessment gaps.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow from introduction through methodology (clear description of transforming type embeddings into contextualized representations) to experiments on CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN and cross-lingual data. The main contributions are stated early (proposing contextualized word-in-context representations and similarity features) and the methodology is described concretely, suggesting consistent notation. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough; one minor presentation caveat is that the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables/examples are provided or evaluated, so their effectiveness is unclear.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces a concrete new method for turning context-agnostic type embeddings into contextualized word-in-context representations via context-derived masks (column-wise max/min/mean and element-wise multiplication) and uses novel word-context similarity features to detect lexical entailment. It also releases two novel datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB and CONTEXT-WN) and demonstrates clear empirical gains, including a new state-of-the-art on Shwartz and Dagan (2015), distinguishing it from prior context-agnostic and PPDB-specific baselines. While the technical components are relatively simple and build on existing embeddings/Context2Vec ideas, their combination and application to contextual lexical entailment represent a notable and well-differentiated contribution rather than a purely incremental rehash.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses lexical entailment in context, a central topic in contemporary NLP, and proposes contextualized representations plus similarity features while introducing two novel datasets and achieving SOTA on a prior benchmark, which fits top-tier conference scope. Its methodology (transforming word embeddings with context-derived masks, using similarity features, and training a supervised classifier), evaluation (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN, cross-lingual experiments), and metrics (weighted/Macro F1) match expectations for rigor at such venues. The work is targeted to the right audience, compares to appropriate baselines (Baroni et al. 2012; Shwartz & Dagan 2015; Context2Vec), and is positioned well within the lexical semantics and contextual representation literature.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Baroni et al., 2012 concatenation baseline; Shwartz & Dagan, 2015 PPDB-specific features; Context2Vec) across multiple datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN, and a cross-lingual set) using comparable evaluation metrics (F1 variants). Comparisons appear fair and reproducible (same datasets/metrics, hyperparameters reported), the authors explicitly report when prior methods perform well or poorly (e.g., establishing new SOTA on Shwartz & Dagan 2015 and noting Context2Vec underperforms), and related work coverage is described as thorough with clear analysis of differences and limitations.

main_claims: Propose transforming context-agnostic word type embeddings into contextualized word-in-context representations and using word-context similarity features to detect lexical entailment in context.

Contextualized representations and similarity features significantly improve performance over context-agnostic baselines on two novel datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB and CONTEXT-WN) and in a cross-lingual setting.
The proposed features are sensitive to word-sense changes signaled by context and capture the directionality of entailment.
Their approach establishes a new state-of-the-art on an existing dataset of semantic relations in context (Shwartz and Dagan, 2015) while relying only on features induced from raw text.
_summary.methodology: Starting from existing word-type embeddings (e.g., GloVe), they construct contextualized word representations by applying context-derived masks (using column-wise max, min, and mean over context embedding matrices and element-wise multiplication) and compute various word-context similarity features (including features derived from Context2Vec); these features (concatenated representations and similarities) are used to train a supervised logistic regression classifier to predict lexical entailment in context.
datasets: cross-lingual lexical entailment dataset (Vyas and Carpuat, 2016)
baselines: Shwartz and Dagan (2015) PPDB-specific feature baseline
limitations: Directly using Context2Vec context representations (~cl,c2v and ~cr,c2v) to add contextual information performs poorly (reported F ≈ 60).
They do not tune classifier parameters except for adding class weights in CONTEXT-WN experiments.
Authors note that richer features (e.g., second-order comparisons) and asymmetric scoring functions may be useful, implying current features have limitations in capturing some relation types.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - lexical entailment / lexical semantics in context
